title: How Brooke Briggs Built Two Heart-Centered Businesses While Raising Kids
guestBio: Brooke Briggs is an educator and entrepreneur who founded both OC Farm School and Magic Steps Music. She specializes in creating meaningful, nature-based learning experiences that foster child development and strengthen family connections through sensory-rich education and outdoor play.
excerpt: Discover how educator-entrepreneur Brooke Briggs built two thriving businesses centered on children's development while navigating motherhood. Her journey from music classes to farm-based learning offers inspiring insights for purpose-driven women.
metaDescription: Learn how Brooke Briggs balances motherhood with running OC Farm School and Magic Steps Music, creating meaningful work that serves children and community.
keyTakeaways: Passion and purpose can grow side by side when you align your business with your values.
Building community requires invisible labor that often goes unrecognized but creates lasting impact.
Sensory-rich learning and outdoor play are essential for children's development in today's digital world.
You can successfully balance motherhood and business ownership when your work feels meaningful.
Creating connections to nature and rhythm helps children develop authentic relationships with themselves and others.
Family bonding happens naturally when parents and children engage in shared learning experiences.
Purpose-driven businesses that serve community needs can thrive while making a difference.

pullQuotes.text: Brooke's work is rooted in connection — to nature, to rhythm, and to the soul of childhood.

timestamps.label: Magic Steps Music and early childhood development
The origin story of OC Farm School
The importance of sensory learning and outdoor play

content:
What happens when you combine a deep love for children's development with an entrepreneurial spirit and the demands of motherhood? You get Brooke Briggs, a woman who has masterfully woven together her passions to create not one, but two thriving businesses that serve families and community in profound ways.

In this heartwarming episode of The Manage Her, host Aimee Rickabus sits down with Brooke to explore her journey as the founder of both Magic Steps Music and OC Farm School. This conversation isn't just about business success—it's about creating meaningful work that aligns with your deepest values while navigating the beautiful complexity of motherhood.

Finding Your Sweet Spot: Where Passion Meets Purpose

Brooke's story begins with a simple but powerful realization: children thrive when they're connected to nature, to rhythm, and to the authentic essence of childhood. Her work through Magic Steps Music demonstrates how early childhood development flourishes when families come together in joyful, music-filled environments. These aren't just classes—they're spaces where parents and children create lasting bonds while engaging in activities that support crucial developmental milestones.

What makes Brooke's approach so compelling is her understanding that learning doesn't happen in isolation. When families participate together in musical experiences, children absorb not just the rhythms and melodies, but the joy and connection that comes from shared discovery. This philosophy has become the foundation for everything she builds, proving that businesses rooted in authentic connection have the power to transform communities.

Growing Something Beautiful: The Birth of OC Farm School

The transition from music education to farm-based learning might seem like a leap, but for Brooke, it was a natural evolution. OC Farm School represents her commitment to reconnecting children with the earth and providing the kind of sensory-rich learning experiences that today's kids desperately need. In our increasingly digital world, the opportunity to dig in dirt, observe growing plants, and experience the cycles of nature becomes not just valuable, but essential.

Through OC Farm School, Brooke has created something that goes beyond traditional education. She's crafted an environment where children can develop authentic relationships with the natural world while building confidence, curiosity, and wonder. The farm becomes a classroom where learning happens through touch, observation, and genuine engagement with living systems.

This hands-on approach to education addresses a crucial gap in many children's lives. When kids can plant seeds, watch them grow, and understand where their food comes from, they develop a deeper appreciation for the interconnectedness of life. Brooke recognized this need and had the courage to create a solution that serves families in her community while building a sustainable business.

The Invisible Labor of Building Community

One of the most powerful aspects of this conversation centers on something that rarely gets discussed in entrepreneurship circles: the invisible labor required to build and maintain community. Brooke's work extends far beyond the official hours of classes and programs. She's creating spaces where families can connect, where children can thrive, and where community bonds are strengthened.

This kind of work—the emotional labor, the relationship building, the countless small actions that create belonging—often goes unrecognized. Yet it's the foundation upon which meaningful businesses are built. Brooke's willingness to embrace this aspect of her work demonstrates a mature understanding of what it takes to create lasting impact.

For women entrepreneurs, this conversation offers permission to value and price the full scope of what you bring to your work. Community building isn't a nice-to-have addition to your business—it's often the very thing that makes your work irreplaceable and transformational.

Balancing Act: Motherhood Meets Business Ownership

Perhaps the most relatable aspect of Brooke's journey is her honest exploration of balancing motherhood with business ownership. Running two purpose-driven businesses while raising children requires a particular kind of grace and flexibility. Brooke's approach shows that this balance isn't about perfect time management or having it all figured out—it's about creating work that feels so aligned with your values that the challenges become worthwhile.

When your business serves families and children, the line between personal and professional naturally blurs. Brooke has learned to navigate this integration in ways that enhance rather than complicate her role as a mother. Her children get to see her building something meaningful, contributing to community, and following her passions—lessons that extend far beyond any classroom.

This integration also means that Brooke understands her audience deeply. As a mother herself, she knows what families need, what children respond to, and what gaps exist in traditional educational approaches. This insider knowledge becomes a significant advantage in creating programs and experiences that truly serve.

The Ripple Effect of Meaningful Work

What emerges from this conversation is a beautiful picture of how meaningful work creates ripple effects that extend far beyond immediate business metrics. When Brooke creates a space for families to connect through music, she's not just teaching songs—she's strengthening family bonds and creating positive childhood memories. When she provides farm-based learning experiences, she's not just teaching about plants—she's fostering environmental stewardship and sensory development.

This kind of impact-driven approach to business offers a compelling alternative to traditional entrepreneurship models. Instead of asking what market you can capture, Brooke asked what community she could serve. Instead of focusing solely on scalability, she prioritized sustainability and depth of connection.

For women considering their own entrepreneurial journeys, Brooke's story provides both inspiration and practical insight. You don't have to choose between meaningful work and business success. You don't have to sacrifice family time for professional growth. What you do need is the courage to build something that aligns with your deepest values and serves a genuine need in your community.

Brooke Briggs represents the kind of leadership that emerges when women trust their instincts, follow their passions, and commit to serving others. Her journey reminds us that the most fulfilling businesses often grow from the intersection of personal passion and community need, creating work that nourishes both the entrepreneur and everyone she serves.

quiz.title: What's Your Purpose-Driven Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally combine your passions with impact, just like Brooke Briggs did when building her heart-centered businesses while raising her children.

questions.question: When you imagine your ideal work life, what excites you most?

options.text: Creating spaces where people feel genuinely connected and supported
Building something sustainable that will have lasting positive impact
Pioneering solutions to problems that others haven't tackled yet
Doing work that perfectly aligns with what you believe matters most

questions.question: How do you typically approach big decisions in your life?

options.text: I consider how it will affect the people and communities I care about
I think through the long-term implications and build from there
I trust my gut instinct about what feels right to explore
I ask myself if it aligns with my core values and priorities

questions.question: What frustrates you most about traditional business approaches?

options.text: They often prioritize individual success over community wellbeing
They focus on short-term gains instead of sustainable growth
They stick to what's always been done instead of innovating
They compromise values for profit and conventional metrics

questions.question: When you're feeling most fulfilled as a leader, you're usually:

options.text: Facilitating meaningful connections between people
Steadily building systems that serve others well
Exploring new territory that others haven't ventured into
Living out your purpose in tangible, daily ways

questions.question: How do you prefer to measure success in your work?

options.text: By the strength and depth of relationships and community I've helped create
By the solid foundation and lasting impact I've built over time
By the innovative solutions and new possibilities I've opened up
By how authentically my work reflects my values and serves my purpose

questions.question: What draws you most to entrepreneurship or leadership roles?

options.text: The opportunity to bring people together around something meaningful
The chance to create something substantial and well-built from the ground up
The freedom to pursue ideas and approaches that feel innovative
The ability to design work that honors both your values and your life

results.title: The Community Weaver
results.description: You have a gift for bringing people together and creating spaces where authentic connections flourish. Like Brooke with her music classes, you understand that the most meaningful work happens when you're building community around shared values. You thrive when your leadership creates ripple effects that touch multiple generations. This episode will inspire you with practical ways to scale your community-building gifts while staying true to your heart-centered approach.

results.title: The Steady Builder
results.description: You believe in growing something beautiful over time, laying strong foundations that will serve others for years to come. Your leadership style focuses on sustainable systems and deep roots rather than quick wins. You understand that meaningful impact requires patience and intentional choices. Brooke's journey from music teacher to farm school founder will resonate deeply with your approach to building purpose-driven work that aligns with your values and grows alongside your life.

results.title: The Intuitive Pioneer
results.description: You trust your instincts about what the world needs and aren't afraid to create something entirely new to meet that need. Your leadership is driven by a deep sense of purpose and willingness to forge your own path. Like Brooke recognizing children's need for nature-based learning, you see gaps others miss and feel called to fill them. This episode will validate your pioneering spirit and show you how to balance bold vision with practical family life.

results.title: The Values-First Visionary
results.description: Everything you do flows from your core beliefs about what matters most. You refuse to compromise your values for conventional success and instead create work that feels deeply meaningful. Your leadership inspires others because it comes from an authentic place of service. Brooke's story of aligning business with family values while making a real difference will speak directly to your heart and give you permission to lead in your own authentic way.

transcript:
Aimee: Let's have some fun. Today, I'm so excited to welcome someone I've known since way back, like sixth grade back. Brooke Briggs was a friend of my little brother, Paul, growing up, and to see what she's built since those early days is nothing short of extraordinary.

Brooke is the founder of Magic Steps Music, a beloved early childhood music program that's touched thousands of families. She's also the founder of OC Farm School, a nature-based educational program that brings kids back to Earth, to curiosity, and to hands-on learning. She's turned a love for music and children into an absolute empire, one built on passion, creativity, and deep service to her community.

Brooke is also a mom of two, a 13-year-old son, and a daughter who's off to college. And she's navigated entrepreneurship as a single parent with heart and grit. She's had a sneak peek at the manager and I can't wait to talk about building, balancing, and staying connected to purpose, especially when the work we do shapes little lives.

All right, Brooke, let's start at the beginning. You and I go way back. For those who are just meeting you, how did Magic Steps Music begin? What inspired you to create it?

Brooke: I have to thank you so much for that lovely and kind introduction. My heart is so full. I was working in the corporate world and I had my daughter and was planning on going back to the corporate world. From the minute they placed that baby on my chest I just knew that I couldn't go back to working 40 hours a week away from her. So I was just so inspired to create something and become some sort of entrepreneur so that I could be home with her.

I put that out into the universe and said my prayers and said, "Okay, I'm open to whatever God you have for me." I'm open to all the signs. When she was only 3 months old, there was a local music class. I enrolled her in that class and it was only the very first class that I just knew right then - wow, this is it.

First I was just amazed by how she was responding at such a young age to the music. She was only 2 months old and she was waving her little hands and kicking her feet. You could tell she was enjoying it and just loving every second of it. So after seeing her response to it, I went home that day and went online and just really wanted to learn more about how I could support her musical growth. On the website it said "become a teacher" and I was just like this is it. This is what I'm going to do.

I got certified to become a music teacher and had no experience at all. So it was simple - it was a 3-day certification. I went there and basically they would teach us about childhood development and how music supports your child's musical growth. And then there would be a test at the end and they'd make sure that you could sing in tune and keep a beat.

But it was really intimidating because when I went to this training, there were people that were Juilliard graduates or had been musicians their whole lives. And so that was really intimidating because I was like, "Oh, I just came from the corporate world. I have no experience in music besides the fact that I love to sing and did choirs in high school." But they were like, "That's fine. That's okay. If you love kids, you love to sing." I was able to pass the test and get certified.

And then I started teaching, but that was, gosh, 17 years ago, right? So I started teaching this other program, this internationally research-based music and movement program and I taught that for years and years and years. And it was only a few years ago that I was like you know what, I'm ready to write my own music. That's kind of how it started. I was going to say it short but ended up being a lot longer. That's kind of how it started.

Aimee: That's amazing. And then so you basically at some point you're like I need to write my own music like this stuff, you know, you've been singing the same songs for over a decade.

Brooke: Yeah, exactly. So really writing the music came from wanting to serve the community better. So the parents appreciated music, similar bands that I appreciated as well. And so this other music program is wonderful, but the music was very traditional and old school. And so it needed something more current. And I saw the need for it. So that's when it was really like I need to - I think I could do this. I need to start writing my own music. But again, there was just so much fear behind that. So it was years before I actually said, you know what, I'm going to do this.

I was at a friend's house just hanging out and her brother was there, somebody I didn't know, and we just started talking and as you do, a new person, you're asking them where they live and getting to know them. And I asked him or he asked me what I did for a living. And then I said, you know, I'm a music teacher. And he's like, oh, I'm a music producer.

Aimee: Yeah.

Brooke: For kids music and I'm just moving back from Japan. I've been in Japan this whole time writing music or producing music for Disney and all these...

Aimee: So, it's just the universe just completely put it in your lap once you were putting it out there like telling your friends, I'm doing it. I'm doing it. I'm doing it. And the universe was like, yeah, heck yeah, you're doing this.

Brooke: Exactly. And it was magical. And then he said, "Well, how about we start in 2 days."

Aimee: Wow.

Brooke: "And I'll come to your house and we'll start, you know, we'll start working on it and I'll just record from your house. We'll start it that way."

Aimee: Wow.

Brooke: And I had started to write. So I had a couple songs. He came over and we knocked out those two songs in one session and they were just so fun and I just was like, "Oh my goodness, the kids are going to absolutely love these." And it was so easy. It just all - you know when things are meant to be, they just unfold so organically and so effortlessly, right? And it's just confirmation like, "Oh, this is what I'm supposed to be doing, you know?"

Aimee: Yeah. Once you let go of your fear, the whole thing just bloomed.

Brooke: Yeah. Wow. Yeah. So and he has been amazing to work with. It's just the chemistry that you have with some people is undeniable and you can't even believe it because you just get along so well. Your creativity flows so well. And so I couldn't do it without him. I feel like I mean I write the music but then we - it's just such a collaborative thing between the two of us.

Aimee: Absolutely.

Brooke: And so yeah, you know, I can't believe we're now on our sixth album.

Aimee: That gives a lot of meaning and purpose and is really important for childhood development and good for families. Something sweet and beautiful to the world.

Brooke: Absolutely. I mean these music classes are so educational for the little ones. I mean music supports speech development among all areas of development - emotional, social, fine motor skills, gross motor skills, everything. I mean it's pretty amazing how not only are these little classes fun but that they're actually getting so much out of them. It's really fun.

Aimee: I mean well I did them with all the kids. I mean, baby Julian and baby Gabriel and baby Daniel and now Indie is doing OC Farm School and she just loves it. And baby Paul's the one who's next. And I can't wait for him. He's going to love it so much. He's so musical.

And it's a really cool way for us to just get to be in that childhood place with our kids, which you rarely get to be, especially as a mom. A lot of times we're just in that mom boss mode, you know, get her done mode. And it's like to be able to just be in a place of play and to be in that beginner's mind of like, okay, I've never played with this little wood thing with the thing that you rub it on. There's so many cool instruments and you're just back in preschool right alongside them.

Brooke: Yes. And play is how we learn. I mean, for children, play is how we learn. And so it really does support a parent child bond and inspires more play. And it's been so rewarding to see and the little ones they come into class and you know they know all the songs and it's just been really so exciting.

Aimee: I know Indie just loves farm school. She's just a big huge fan. So then farm school's totally separate like a completely other business that was inspired because not only do you love music and this goes with my kids too right Sydney my daughter - she's the inspiration behind all of it like she's the musical girl and then she loved animals and I mean I love animals as well, wanting to give her an education with animals and inspire learning, but learning about animals and being outdoors and all that. So that's how the whole farm school came. So, how did that happen? What were you thinking? How did this happen?

Brooke: In business, you know, if there's a need for something and it's not out there, that's your sign to create it, you know? So, I wanted my daughter to have a little class where she learned about animals and farming and they didn't have that. So, I went into my local petting zoo here and I was like, you know, could I do a little class here? It was as simple as that. And they were so accepting of it. You know, with and also that I'd already - that I had an established business and that I worked with kids in the community doing music classes and so they were really supportive of it.

And yeah, and so that started and it's just been so incredible to watch that program grow and to hire other people that have animal science degrees and really know their stuff about animals and farming and all the things. But I mean the little ones they're so little - the farm school is for - I mean I do a caregiver and me program for little ones ages 18 months to three and then we do the older program for ages 3 to seven. And so that's really what we're focused on is the younger ones, the little ones.

So really, it's as simple as teaching them where our food comes from. Because once a month you guys go to the farm and they get to pick their own strawberries and their sweet peas right off of the vine. And they're like, "Oh my gosh, this is where my food comes from." And we do cooking and it's really establishing these real healthy eating habits from an early age.

And so there's that. And then animals and these animals give us so much with cows that give us milk that we use in cheese and all the things, but then also being around animals - it's really nurturing compassion and kindness and I really think that if you learn to have that at a young age with animals it really also carries into how you treat people. I think that's really important.

Aimee: So yeah, it's so sweet. It really is. It's like, how come no one thought of this before? It's amazing. Here we are with our petting zoo and our organic farm. We're kind of lucky to live in a town like this, but I'm sure there are other moms out there that are living in towns that have a petting zoo, an organic farm who could reach out to Brooke and figure out how to do this. You can do this, too.

Brooke: Exactly. We are in the process. So I have an amazing operations manager and her name is Jennifer and again talk about having these incredible people - you know what is that saying - it takes the team to make the dream?

Aimee: Yeah, it takes the team to make the dream.

Brooke: Yeah exactly. So you know she is so incredible and we're working on licensing the program. 

Aimee: Oh, licensing the program. I love that because you guys have done all the work because they don't need to recreate it. The curriculum is done. They can come to you, get a license, and do this all over the US. Anybody who's passionate about early childhood development, animals, music, little kids - this is something that women can do and free up their lives and get out of the corporate rat race and be their own boss. I love this. It's so cool. Such a gift.

Brooke: So, we're excited about licensing the farm school program. To give other people this opportunity and really to just spell it all out for them - here is how you do it and successfully because there was a learning curve for us and figuring out what works and what doesn't work. So, we're excited about that and as well as licensing the music program as well. So, we're excited about Magic Steps Music program.

Aimee: So you'll have a 3-day course where people get certified to do it. That's amazing.

Brooke: We definitely will do a training and regular training and support and give them all the songs and the materials and yeah, I mean, it's going to be so professional and so well done so that everybody can be as successful as we've been.

Aimee: That's amazing, Brooke. Look at you go, girl. I'm excited. I'm so proud of you. You know, you've come a long way in a decade.

Brooke: Thank you.

Aimee: Yeah, it's been a rough one and you just pushed through and made it, you know, you turned lemons into lemonade, didn't you?

Brooke: Yeah. I mean, my advice to people that have an idea is don't let anybody tell you that you can't actually make a living at it. Because when I started this music company, people were saying, you know, it's only going to buy you groceries. That's it. If I had really listened to them, I wouldn't be where I'm at today. And I do believe that things are blessed when they're for the greater good of all.

Aimee: Absolutely. I so agree.

Brooke: So I - you know it was always my passion was always to serve and give back and then it just ended up becoming a career that I could support my family on. And so I'm so grateful for that - that I've been able to support my two kiddos and also serve the community in this way.

Aimee: You really have. You really stepped up as a mom. You really did. You have taken such good care of your kids using music to do it. It's been really amazing to watch you do that. It's very inspiring. So cool.

Brooke: Thank you.

Aimee: So, you've created something that's lasted and grown. What were some of the first steps you took to make it real? Did you have a business plan or support or just drive? I think this is like OC farm school. Did you have a business plan or what did you do? How did you do this?

Brooke: You know, I would recommend that everybody have a business plan. But I'm - you know, it really wasn't - there was no business plan. It was just all in my mind. The vision was all in my mind of okay, this is what I want to do. And so yeah. So I went in with the intention of let's start a little class and I just - every idea that I had I just implemented it and it evolved into what it is now through trial and error or what the people need.

So farm school really started as a camp and then from the camp then there was - everybody needed an outdoor school. And so then it was, okay, we're going to be a kindergarten readiness program. We're going to not only teach them about animals, but we're going to teach them how to write their name because they're young. So, we're going to try to fit it all in. So, we were teaching them how to write their names and their letters and their letter sounds and as much as we could to get them prepared for kindergarten, first grade.

But then when everything opened up again, we were kind of a bit of a relief that we didn't have to do all that. We could just really focus on what it was meant to be originally, which was teaching them about where their food comes from and about animals and the compassion for animals, and it being an enrichment program.

Aimee: Absolutely. But that's that AC that I talk about in the book, the adjusting constantly. You did the AC there to compensate because you had a bunch of kids that needed to learn.

Brooke: Yeah.

Aimee: And there was nowhere to go. Pivot, pivot, pivot.

Brooke: Pivot. I mean, the music program when I started, I did start with a company that gave us just kind of the how-to on how to start a business, which you can find that right now - I mean, chat GPT basically, right? These are the steps. Get a business license. Da da da. So, it did help me out. This company helped me out getting started with the music program.

People also give you ideas on okay, do it this way. But you have to, as a business owner, also lean into your own intuitions and be like, you know what, I'm going to think outside the box and I'm going to do something maybe a little bit of what I want to do. So, I ended up with the music, I ended up tweaking some things and doing things that I wanted to do versus what that company had told me to do.

Aimee: Yes. Totally. I noticed that. I mean, I remember when you were at that point, you know, kind of making - putting your own spin on their brand. I remember those days.

Brooke: Yeah. Kind of making it a little bit more yours.

Aimee: Yes.

Brooke: And thinking outside the box. I mean they might have said you need to rent things out of community centers and you need to do this and I was like no you know what I want to do my own thing and I want to rent my own space and I don't know - that's the great thing about being an entrepreneur is that you can do your own thing too, make your own decisions. Which is so nice. It's nice to be able to have that control over things. Be like no this would work better for me. This would work better for the business, for everyone.

Aimee: What's one thing you'd tell another single mom who wants to start something of her own?

Brooke: Believe in yourself. Believe in yourself. You can do it. I mean, and know that the universe will - if you put out there this is what I want to do. I think that it all just falls into place for you, right? So I would tell single moms to just believe in themselves and to take baby steps, right? Baby steps into the goal and one step at a time, conquering what the dream is.

And it can be overwhelming, you know, taking care of children and creating a business. And it doesn't happen overnight. It definitely, for me it was just steady growth, but over time, you just continue to keep believing yourself and keep making the effort. And yeah, it's really cool when you look back at 10 years and you go, "Oh my goodness, where I am now," but it was because those little baby steps and continuing to put in the effort.

Aimee: Absolutely. Yeah. The little things add up.

Brooke: Yeah, the little things add up.

Aimee: I always say it's not one big thing. It's always a bunch of little things that get you there.

Brooke: It is. Yeah. So, don't get overwhelmed by doing something big because there's no one big thing. No, there's a bunch of little steps. They get to the little steps. It's all about the little steps.

Aimee: Like the magic steps in your music program. I was like, there's your magic steps right there.

Brooke: Exactly. Exactly. Love that.

Aimee: And so you're in a space that's part education, part entertainment, and part wellness. How do you stay creative and inspired over the long haul?

Brooke: Oh my goodness. Well, I think that breaks, vacations.

Aimee: Oh, yeah.

Brooke: Away from it all has always brought me back to my work, feeling inspired and all the things. So, you know, when the kids are out of school, I take time off with them to go out in nature and to disconnect. And that's always been how I stay so incredibly focused and driven and excited is those breaks.

Aimee: Yeah. Having boundaries.

Brooke: Having and disconnecting and having - we're a big outdoorsy family, so we go to Yosemite or somewhere there's no Wi-Fi and really just connecting with nature and the kids and it's really inspiring.

Aimee: I love that about you guys. You guys have the best adventures. What was your last adventure? What was the last adventure?

Brooke: Well, I'm thinking of the - I guess I just was in Kauai with my son on a surf trip.

Aimee: Oh, how fun.

Brooke: Yeah. So, I guess that was the last one during spring break.

Aimee: Amazing. Oh my gosh. And what's next?

Brooke: And then we're going to Greece. My daughter and I are going to Greece and taking her girlfriend in June.

Aimee: Yeah. In a couple weeks. So, you know, I do think that the traveling is really what helps give me a lot of the inspiration and the creativity.

Brooke: Yeah. Totally. Yeah. It's neat to be in different places and also to have that time to bond with your kids, too.

Aimee: Yeah, absolutely. And it doesn't have to be an expensive trip to Europe. It can be just camping or a little weekend getaway at an Airbnb or I don't know. It can be that easy, too, right? Just making the time to get away and get out of the ordinary.

Brooke: Yeah. Sometimes it's just a little break from the ordinary.

Aimee: Yeah. It's so simple. I hopped in - I threw the kids in the car and we drove to Arizona a couple weeks ago and we just went to the wave pool with my brother Paul and Gabe got to go fishing on the lake and it was 3 days but the kids felt like a world away from everything.

Brooke: Yeah, it's nice to get a little time every once in a while.

Aimee: Absolutely.

Brooke: In the stillness that you also get these creative ideas and you're like, "Oh, I'm going to do this when I get back and I'm going to do this." And it's in that stillness. And also, if you can have a little bit of solitude, you know, I know that's really hard with kids, but it's the best.

Aimee: You're right. It is so important. Solitude, you know, because us women - I mean men too, not to take this away from men. I mean if you love to serve and give, we give give give and it's a beautiful thing and to give and I love giving and I get so much back from giving. But you got to love yourself to love others well. And so if you give yourself a little bit of solitude, it's amazing how much energy just a little bit. A day.

Brooke: A day. I feel like a day is like a whole week. Used to be when I was single. 24 hours. There's been times where I've just - I know it's kind of crazy because I'm just single female. Would go to Joshua Tree and I would stay there for 24 hours and I would come home and I would feel like a completely different person as if I'd just been on a vacation for a week only from 24 hours of solitude. It's amazing what that can give to you and how much creativity and energy you can get back from 24 hours or a day or something.

Aimee: Wonderful advice. You know, so many of us it's like we're trying to prioritize all of these things, our kids, our jobs, our husbands, you know, our partners, and then somewhere along the line ourselves. And we really do have to put ourselves up on that priority list a little bit. And you know, maybe try that, girls. Maybe try taking a day for yourself. When was the last time you had a date for yourself?

Brooke: I don't remember. Honestly, and I think that people feel guilty like I'm selfish if I do this. And they might have a hard time telling their husbands that they need this time. But I say just try it one time and your husbands or your partners or your kids will feel a difference from you because you're able to give back more because you were able to love yourself first.

So it's hard though because I know how I love serving. I love taking care of my family and it's not easy to give yourself that love. It's not. But once you do it one time and you're like, "Oh my gosh." Okay. I need to do this.

Aimee: I've never done this. I've never even thought about doing it. And now I'm like, "Okay, I'm curious." You have me curious, what happens when I'm alone for 24 hours?

Brooke: I asked a friend. And I was like, "Do you think that I'm crazy if I just go to Joshua Tree in the desert for 24 hours?" Another mom. But she had been an outdoorsy adventure before kids. I hadn't, right? So, she had backpacked by herself, the whole thing. And she's like, "Girl, do it. You're going to love it." I was like, "Okay." So, I kind of felt a little crazy but she was so right. And when I did that, I came back and that was just the first of - And then I went backpacking.

Aimee: How often do you do this solo thing? What's your ritual?

Brooke: I would say I mean, well, it's all over the place, right? But every 6 months.

Aimee: I love you. Wow. Every six months. I love that. That's wild. Even just 24 hours. That's amazing. That is so amazing.

Brooke: I have a boyfriend now and so I have kids and stuff so I love to spend time with him of course. If nobody's going to go, then I think you just go and know that you're supposed to go by yourself and it just fills your cup.
